Project Name: GS12856-VPA-3 Unlocking Communities Water Filters for Households
Description: The voluntary project activity (VPA) is a voluntary initiative by Unlocking Communities (UC) to distribute household water filters in Haiti. The target beneficiaries under the project will be the households which were earlier dependent on boiling using non-renewable biomass for safe drinking water. Unlocking Communities is a locally run organization working in northern and central Haiti. Distribution of household water filters which is a clean energy product as compared to the baseline, will reduce the GHG emissions by eliminating the need for water treatment through boiling.  The use of water filters will offer several environmental and health benefits such as reduction in the levels of indoor air pollution (IAP), reduction in water borne diseases, deforestation etc. thereby having a positive impact on the health of the community.

Project Name: GS12856-VPA-2 Unlocking Communities Water Filters for Institutions
Description: The voluntary project activity (VPA) is a voluntary initiative by Unlocking Communities (UC) to distribute institution water filters in Haiti. The target beneficiaries under the project will be the institutions like schools which were earlier dependent on boiling using non-renewable biomass for safe drinking water. Unlocking Communities is a locally run organization working in northern and central Haiti. Distribution of institutional water filters which is a clean energy product as compared to the baseline, will reduce the GHG emissions by eliminating the need for water treatment through boiling.  The use of water filters will offer several environmental and health benefits such as reduction in the levels of indoor air pollution (IAP), reduction in water borne diseases, deforestation etc. thereby having a positive impact on the health of the community.

Project Name: Kuphatana - Acções Inclusivas para Florestas Sustentáveis ​​em Moçambique
Description:
The Kuphatana project aims to reforest 1,000 hectares of highly degraded land in the Administrative Post of Murraça, Mozambique, to restore the local ecosystem and support the socio-economic development of nearby communities. The project adopts a participatory approach, integrating environmental restoration with diversified livelihood activities such as beekeeping and dried mango production to ensure long-term sustainability and community engagement.
Climate change mitigation/adaptation contributions
Carbon Sequestration: The project establishes a forest that serves as a carbon sink, sequestering an annual average of 8,925 tCO2e. Over the 40-year crediting period, the total estimated net benefit is 351,044.5 tCO2e.
Biodiversity Enhancement: By planting a mix of native species (Vachellia xanthophloea and Khaya anthotheca) alongside non-invasive Eucalyptus urophylla, the project restores habitats for local flora and fauna
Climate Adaptation through Livelihoods - Beekeeping: The project engages 15 families per year from adjacent villages, providing them with beehives and training. This creates a direct economic incentive for the community to protect the forest from threats such as uncontrolled fires or illegal deforestation, as the bee colonies rely on the trees for nectar.
Climate Adaptation through Livelihoods - Sustainable Value Chains: Establishing a local value chain for dried mangoes offers diversified, climate-resilient financial growth for local families.

Project Name: GS12856-VPA-1 Unlocking Communities High Efficiency Charcoal Cookstove for Households
Description: The voluntary project activity (VPA) is a voluntary initiative by Unlocking Communities (UC) to distribute improved charcoal stoves in Haiti. The target beneficiaries under the project will be the households which were earlier dependent on charcoal based traditional stoves for cooking. Unlocking Communities is a locally run organization working in northern and central Haiti. Distribution of improved cookstove which is a clean energy product as compared to the baseline, will reduce the GHG emissions.  The use of improved cookstove will offer several environmental and health benefits such as reduction in the levels of indoor air pollution (IAP), women drudgery, deforestation etc. thereby having a positive impact on the health of the community.

Project Name: GS13044 GS13045 RVPA-1 Kenya LifeStraw Give Back Program
Description: The project addresses the lack of safe drinking water in certain schools in Kenya and the resulting negative impacts on students’ health and learning. The projects will distribute, install, and service low greenhouse gases (GHG) emitting water purification systems at point-of-collection (POC) to provide safe drinking water (SDW) for institutional application at schools (mostly, primary and secondary schools; other educational institutions can be included). The water purification systems will reduce both the use of and demand for firewood and other fuels used to boil water for drinking, leading to a reduction and/or demand for carbon dioxide emissions. The project aims to provide water treatment to Schools in Kenya to address microbiological contamination and reduce the energy demand and respective carbon emissions from the population served by the project activity for whom the common practice of water treatment is or would have been water boiling. The project accounts for purified water consumed for drinking.

Project Name: Biogas for Households (FIT-B4H)
Project Developer Name: Fair Climate Fund India Private Limited
Description:
The B4H Initiative  is a sustainable energy project aimed at promoting renewable energy and waste management in rural households across two Indian states. The initiative involves the installation of 14,690 household biogas plants, primarily in Maharashtra (13,752 units) and Madhya Pradesh (938 units). These plants utilize animal dung to generate biogas for thermal energy, reducing greenhouse gas emissions and dependence on fossil fuels.

The project adheres to the "Methodology for Animal Manure Management and Biogas Use for Thermal Energy Generation" (Version 1.1, released January 25, 2023). The Household biogas units are sized at 2m³ , 3m³ , and 4m³, enabling clean cooking and organic fertilizer production for beneficiaries.

By addressing methane emissions from manure and providing affordable energy, B4H enhances livelihoods, improves air quality, and supports climate resilience in underserved communities. The initiative targets rural farmers, with installations verified through a detailed beneficiary database,to ensure long-term monitoring for carbon credit issuance.

Project Name: Katunguru Restoration Project
Description:
The project aims to restore 791 hectares of degraded land in the north-eastern section of Kibale National Park, Uganda. Following heavy encroachment in the past century, the area was abandoned but natural forest regeneration remains severely constrained by elephant grass and recurring fires. Without active intervention, forest recovery would not occur.

The project site is located in the mid-eastern part of Kibale National Park in Kyenjojo District, Western Uganda, and borders communities largely dependent on smallholder agriculture and affected by land scarcity, human–wildlife conflict, and pressure on natural resources.

Restoration will focus on active reforestation using indigenous tree species, including nursery establishment, seedling production and planting, weeding, fire prevention, patrolling, and long-term maintenance. Community members will be engaged through employment opportunities. Complementary activities will address human–wildlife conflict, support alternative livelihoods such as beekeeping, piggery, and agroforestry, and promote environmental awareness.

Without the project, the area would remain dominated by elephant grass and frequent fires, resulting in low carbon storage, limited biodiversity recovery, and soil degradation. The project changes this trajectory by enabling large-scale forest restoration, improving habitat quality, increasing carbon sequestration, and delivering socio-economic benefits to neighbouring communities.

Project Name: QSHM Cork Oak Afforestation Pre 2026
Description:
Transformation of degraded land in Extremadura, Spain into cork oak estates. This project is the first in a series of future projects that will be grouped by annual cohorts.

The baseline scenario is that traditional cork oak woodlands are under serious threats from climate change, fragile ecosystems, lack of management, wildfires as well as biotic and abiotic stresses. There is almost no incentive to plant new cork oak trees due to the very long time horizon.

Cork oak estates increase the ecosystem value of the land, providing valuable habitats for native species, reducing erosion, and preserving the soil structure, increasing water holding and storing capacity as well as increasing shades. Degraded land is often used for non-permanent pastures, leading to desertification.

Project Name: Transforming Rice Cultivation in Kabirdham
Description: The rice cultivation project in Kabirdham, India, is a joint effort between Commonland B.V. and Chhattisgarh Agricon Private Limited, aimed at promoting sustainable agricultural practices while reducing methane emissions. The initiative encourages farmers to adopt Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) and Direct Seeded Rice (DSR) techniques to replace conventional continuous flooding, which is a major source of methane emissions in rice farming. By improving water management and soil conditions, this project seeks to enhance agricultural sustainability and farmer livelihoods.

Project Name: Reducing Methane Emissions through Improved Rice Cultivation in India
Description: Reducing Methane Emissions through Improved Rice Cultivation in India Program of Activities (PoA), developed by Commonland B.V., aims to address one of the key sources of greenhouse gas emissions in agriculture: methane from rice farming. The POA aims to promote sustainable cultivation practices through promoting Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) and Direct Seeded Rice (DSR), as alternatives to conventional continuous flooding. By improving water management and soil conditions, the PoA seeks to reduce methane emissions, enhance climate resilience, and support farmer livelihoods across different regions of India.

Project Name: Scaling up the production and sale of efficient cookstoves in the urban areas of Maputo Province
Description:
The project aims at enhancing the penetration rate of fuel-efficient charcoal fired cookstoves to urban households of Maputo province contributing to the reduction of non-renewable cooking fuel usage. The project also foresees to sustain the local supply chain of cookstoves productions and generate generate new jobs along with other SDGs benefits.
The project is self-financed and additional: carbon revenues are meant to expand distribution activities and guarantee mainteinance throughtout their creditable lifespan.

Project Name: BIOPLANTATION Ghana Regenerative Agroforestry Project
Description: BIOPLANTATION is undertaking an agroforestry project in collaboration with local communities in Ghana. The project activity aims to restore degraded, under utilized land by developing biodiverse, regenerative agro forests. These forests will help combat climate change, support food security and create local jobs. The project will include Moringa Oleifera, a fast-growing and climate-resilient tree and associated species, along with local crops like peanuts and native species, to regenerate soil, store carbon, and support local livelihoods. BIOPLANTATION will bring together proven techniques, in a modular and regenerative design, that can be replicated and scaled up to different communities in Ghana. Planting of trees and crops including Moringa, peanuts, native fruits, fire/windbreak species, and aromatic plants. Soil restoration and regeneration will be through nitrogen-fixing plants and organic matter. Enhancing indigenous biodiversity will be by allocating 10% of project land to support pollination, biodiversity, and honey production.  BIOPLANTATION Ghana is committed to working in close partnership with local communities. During all phases of planning, implementation, and monitoring, community feedback and participation will be prioritized. The project will offer training, ownership, and income opportunities to local residents.

Project Name: Smart Train Lease CO2 Reduction Project
Description:
Smart Train Lease GmbH is a subsidiary of Siemens Mobility founded in 2023 and
launched to the market in February 2024 in Germany. Smart Train Lease offers the
opportunity to rent innovative, sustainable, state-of-the-art battery trains which can
replace diesel trains. Mireo Smart trains are part of the leasing model of Smart Train
Lease. They are available in various versions but for this project activity the focus is on
the highly energy-efficient battery-electric train (referred to as “Plus B”). The battery
train fleet is managed centrally and enables rail operators to rent trains at short notice, offering a cost-efficient solution for regional passenger transport services, depending
on the demand. production, lease and monitoring of 24 Mireo Smart Plus B across Germany, starting from 2026. The ultimate purpose of Smart Train Lease is to displace old
diesel trains, contributing to the cleaner, greener, and smarter mobility transition in
Germany by aligning with sustainable development goals, resulting in a reduction of
10,334 tCO2e on average per year and a total of 51,672 tCO2e over the first crediting
period of 5 years from 01/07/2026 to 30/06/2031.

Project Name: Greenated Small Farmers AgroForestry Project in India
Description:
The objective of the program is to support smallholder farmers and rural communities in adopting sustainable agroforestry practices, which involve planting trees alongside crops or on fallow land across India. These activities will help restore soil health, improve agricultural productivity, and generate long-term environmental and economic benefits.

Farmers will retain full ownership and rights over the trees they plant and the outputs from those trees. Any profits from the planting of additional species (such as fruit or timber trees) will belong entirely to the farmers. In addition to the economic benefits, the project will also reduce vulnerability to climate change. By increasing tree cover, it will help combat land degradation, improve water retention, and enhance biodiversity. Furthermore, the program will provide farmers access to carbon credit revenue share, promoting sustainable land use while improving their long-term livelihoods

Project Name: GS23264-GS23271-RVPA-1 Recovery of metals and minerals from incinerator bottom ash in Switzerland
Description:
The project includes the construction and operation of a metal and mineral recovery plant in Switzerland. The plant has the capacity to process thousands of tons of incinerator bottom ash (IBA) from nearby incinerators on an annual basis. Around half of materials from the incinerator waste are set to be recovered, among which is a significant share of valuable metals and mineral fractions. The recovered metal and mineral fractions are sold downstream and therefore re-enter the economic cycle. The activity results in the reduction of emissions through the replacement of virgin materials in manufacturing, avoiding emissions involved in the extraction and processing of such materials.

RVPA-1 follows the implementation of VPA-1.

Project Name: GS23502 VPA-1 Factory Price Station
Project Developer Name: TAMPEC SOLUCOES EM TECNOLOGIA E LOGISTICA LTDA/Green Mining
Description:
Green Mining’s "GS23502 VPA-1 Factory Price Station" is a Real Case Voluntary Project Activity (VPA) that promotes the structured integration of informal waste collection into a formal recycling chain, with traceability, efficiency and environmental performance, while generating verifiable emission reductions.

The VPA is physically located in Santo Amaro neighborhood, in the municipality of São Paulo, State of São Paulo, Brazil. The core project facilities consist of a waste logistics and aggregation unit where recyclable materials collected by local waste pickers are received, weighed, sorted, temporarily stored and transported to recycling plants, from which they are reintroduced into the production chain.

Project Name: CDR ORINOQUIA
Description:
The CDR ORINOQUÍA project, developed by Biofix Consultoría S.A.S BIC, is a large-scale afforestation, reforestation, and revegetation (ARR) initiative designed to mitigate climate change by restoring degraded savannah ecosystems in the Orinoco region of Colombia. The project covers 8,441 hectares across seven private properties (Arizona, La Fortuna, La Carolina, San Andrés, Villa Claudia, Buenos Aires, and San Miguel) located in Puerto Gaitán, Meta.

The project employs a dual reforestation strategy:

Forest Plantations: Establishment of non-native species (Eucalyptus pellita and Pinus caribaea).

Natural Enrichment: Restoration of non-flood zones and flooded areas (gallery and moriche forests) using native species such as Cassia grandis, Hymenaea courbaril, Caraipa llanorum, Mauritia flexuosa, Euterpe precatoria, and Manilkara zapota.

To achieve its goals, the project operates a permanent nursery producing over three million seedlings annually. Implementation includes specialized machinery for site preparation, alongside GPS and drone technology for precise monitoring. This integrated approach aims to increase carbon sequestration and generate certified carbon credits while contributing to SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 8 (Decent Work), SDG 5 (Gender Equality) and SDG 15 (Life on Land). The project boundary encompasses all land parcels dedicated to these ARR activities as GHG sinks.

Project Name: Green Mining’s GHG Emissions Reductions through Recycling in Brazil
Project Developer Name: TAMPEC SOLUCOES EM TECNOLOGIA E LOGISTICA LTDA/Green Mining
Description:
Green Mining's PoA is based on operationalizing the collection, transportation, and destination of waste for recycling. In doing so, the PoA promotes the circular economy and prevents the environmental impacts of manufacturing new products from virgin material, ensuring greater energy efficiency in the production process and, thus, reducing associated Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions.

The PoA is located in Brazil, and its operations are organized around receiving centers strategically placed in areas with high post-consumer waste generation. These centers receive, weigh, and properly store waste brought by registered local waste pickers, providing them fair compensation in a way that prioritizes the social inclusion of people in vulnerable situations.

In this regard, the PoA stands out for its disruptive nature, passing on the full value of waste to registered waste pickers and expanding the positive social impact of recycling.

Once properly registered, the waste is transported to the plants responsible for the recycling process, ensuring its subsequent reinsertion into the production chain.

This PoA constitutes, therefore, an overarching framework aimed at promoting recycling in Brazil in an inclusive and sustainable manner.

Project Name: Alternative Fuel and Clinker Optimization Project
Description:
The proposed project activity aims to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions at the Lam Thach Cement Plant through the introduction of alternative fuels and clinker optimization measures. The project will replace a portion of fossil fuels traditionally used in the cement kiln with waste-derived fuels such as refuse-derived fuel (RDF) and dewatered sludge, which would otherwise be landfilled or improperly disposed. This substitution reduces CO₂ emissions associated with fossil fuel combustion and supports improved waste management practices.

In addition, the project will lower process emissions by decreasing the clinker-to-cement ratio through the increased use of suitable supplementary cementitious materials. This results in reduced calcination-related CO₂ emissions from clinker production. The combined interventions contribute to a significant reduction in the carbon intensity of cement manufacturing at Lam Thach.

The initiative supports Vietnam’s climate and circular economy objectives by diverting waste from landfills, reducing fossil fuel dependence, and promoting more efficient resource use. The overall project activity contributes to regional and national sustainable development.

Project Name: Next Green Energy Wind Power Project
Description: The Next Green Energy Wind Power Project is a greenfield, grid-connected onshore wind Independent Power Project located near Bekkul-baba village in the Almaty district of Aktobe city, Aktobe Region, Kazakhstan. The project comprises eight utility-scale wind turbines (EN200/6.25), each rated at 6.25 MW, for a total installed capacity of 50 MW, together with a step-up substation, grid interconnection infrastructure, and associated access and site facilities. The project is expected to generate approximately 150,000 MWh of renewable electricity annually for delivery to Kazakhstan’s national electricity grid under a long-term power purchase agreement. By displacing fossil-fuel-based generation on the grid, the project is estimated to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by around 113,550 tCO₂e per year and supports Kazakhstan’s renewable energy and long-term decarbonization objectives.

Project Name: Darmen Shuak Wind Power Project
Description: The Darmen Shuak Wind Power Project is a grid-connected onshore wind energy project located near Bekkul-baba village in the Almaty district of Aktobe city, Kazakhstan. The project comprises eight utility-scale wind turbines with a combined installed capacity of 50 MW, designed to harness the strong and stable wind resources of the Aktobe Region. The project is expected to generate approximately 150,000 MWh of renewable electricity annually and supply it to Kazakhstan’s national grid. By replacing fossil-fuel-based electricity generation, the project is estimated to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by around 113,550 tCO₂e per year and contributes to national energy security, grid reliability, and Kazakhstan’s long-term clean energy transition.

Project Name: Community Carbon Efficient Cooking Programme VPA01: Nepal
Project Developer Name: SINGAPORE TREASURE CARBON TECH PTE. LTD.
Description: The project will install efficient Improved Cook Stoves (ICS) in firewood-dependent households in designated Nepal areas, replacing traditional cookstoves to reduce indoor air pollution and GHG emissions. The VPA will be implemented within several municipalities in Lumbini Province of Nepal.

Project Name: Community Carbon Efficient Cooking Programme --Nepal
Project Developer Name: SINGAPORE TREASURE CARBON TECH PTE. LTD.
Description: The project will install efficient Improved Cook Stoves (ICS) in firewood-dependent households in designated Nepal areas, replacing traditional cookstoves to reduce indoor air pollution and GHG emissions. The PoA will be implemented in Nepal.

Project Name: US SecondCropTM Microbial Carbon Removal (USSC24)
Description:
Overview
The US SecondCropTM Microbial Carbon Removal project represents a pioneering
approach in agriculture-based carbon removal and is centered around the use of
proprietary microbial technology to improve soil health and drive long-term carbon
sequestration. The project transforms conventional farmland into durable carbon sinks
by enhancing stable soil carbon, producing measurable climate and soil health outcomes.
A key innovation in this program is the deployment of CarbonBuilderTM, a proprietary
microbial inoculant developed by Loam Bio (refer to Appendix 5 - CarbonBuilder
Development Stages at Loam Bio for a detailed summary of the product development
pipeline). CarbonBuilder is applied as a seed treatment to enhance plant fertility,
promote soil health, and increase the sequestration of soil organic carbon by
reintroducing beneficial fungi into the soil. The US SecondCrop Microbial Carbon Removal
project represents a grouped project comprising 36 farms. Participating farms are
located in Illinois, Iowa, Minnesota, Nebraska, South Dakota and Wisconsin with a
median farm size of 550 acres. All farms in the project are historically and currently used
for cropping, primarily under a corn-soy rotation.
Prior to the implementation of this project, there were no equivalent initiatives in the
region focused on stable soil organic carbon sequestration. Agricultural practices in the
U.S. Midwest were predominantly conventional, characterized by routine tillage and
heavy reliance on synthetic fertilizers. The baseline scenario assumes the continuation of
these practices in the absence of the project, leading to higher emissions and declining
soil resilience over time. Under the project, participating farmers apply CarbonBuilder to
applicable crops each year at the time of planting. Unlike conventional soil carbon
projects that depend only on practice changes (no-till, cover crops), Loam’s inoculant

accelerates microbial-fungal-root interactions that channel plant carbon into mineral-
associated organic matter (MAOM) and aggregate-associated organic matter.
Building stronger soil health in agricultural landscapes within United
States
Regenerative agricultural practices hold immense potential for higher adoption rates
among farmers in the U.S. Midwest, a region characterized by its flat topography and
fertile mollisol soils that are ideal for large-scale, mechanized farming (USDA NRCS).
Historically, conventional farming in this area has relied on intensive tillage and heavy
use of synthetic fertilizers to maximize yields. However, this has led to higher emissions and declining soil resilience over time. A study in the journal Earth's Future found that
agricultural fields in the Midwestern U.S. have lost an average of two millimeters of soil
per year since Euro-American settlement, nearly double the rate the USDA considers
sustainable. It has been estimated that over the last 160 years, the region has lost
approximately 57.6 trillion metric tons of topsoil, with plowing activities cited as the
major culprit (Thaler et al. 2022). Similarly, research published in the Proceedings of the
National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) revealed that the Midwest Corn Belt has lost its
topsoil (A-horizon soil) from roughly one-third of its cultivated area, a significantly
higher estimate than previous soil surveys indicated. This soil loss has removed 1.4 ±
0.5 petagrams of carbon from hillslopes, reducing crop yields in the study area by
around 6% and causing billions in annual economic losses (Thaler et al. 2021). Poor soil
health has a significant impact on productivity and the broader agricultural economy.
The shift to regenerative practices including reduced or no-tillage, cover cropping, and
the use of bio-stimulants, is therefore crucial for improving soil health, enhancing water
retention, and mitigating climate change through potential widespread carbon removal
and long-term sequestration. The SecondCrop Microbial Carbon Removal project is
designed to accelerate this transition by making it easier for farmers to adopt these
practices over a sustained period.
Microbial seed technology and development of CarbonBuilder
CarbonBuilder harnesses the interactions between beneficial fungi, plants and soil. With
CarbonBuilder, beneficial fungi are reintroduced into soils that have been stripped of
their native fungal communities due to harsh cropping systems. These fungi enhance
natural carbon deposition into stable aggregates and mineral associated forms and
promote long-term retention of carbon in the soil. Loam's approach is rooted in
extensive research and development. The company has collaborated with leading
academic institutions, including the University of Sydney, Australian National University,
Western Sydney University, University of Newcastle, University of Queensland, and
Lawrence Livermore to deepen its understanding of plant-fungi-soil interactions in the
development of CarbonBuilder. CarbonBuilder represents our patented use of Dark
Septate Endophytes (DSE) to establish symbiotic relationships with plant roots resulting
in the extension of fungal networks that improve nutrient uptake, enhance soil structure,
and increase carbon deposition. The fungi within CarbonBuilder actively deposit carbon
into stable soil aggregates and bind it to mineral surfaces increasing the mean residence
time in the soil from weeks or months to decades or even centuries. In addition,
replicated small-plot field trials hold a pivotal place in Loam’s CarbonBuilder product
development process. These trials have allowed us to methodically evaluate
CarbonBuilder's impact on crop yield, soil health, and carbon sequestration. The data generated from these trials enabled Loam to identify the most promising products, which
then progressed to strip trials on farms.
Loam Bio patented technology and geographic operations
Loam Bio and its CarbonBuilder products are USDA compliant to be utilised on-farm in
the United States (South Dakota, Nebraska, Minnesota, Iowa, Missouri, Mississippi,
Arizona; with approvals pending in Ohio, Colorado and Idaho.). To date, Loam Bio has
been successfully granted 10 patents covering fungal compositions and microbial
mixtures isolated in United States and Australia and their methods of use within Loam’s
bioproduction operations. These fungal strains are active ingredients in CarbonBuilder’s
product line which are available for use as eligible management practices under the
SecondCrop program. Loam Bio, LLC continues to actively build out its patent pipeline,
with over 30 patents that have been filed within jurisdictions of Australia, Brazil, Canada
and United States.
Loam Bio as project developer
Loam Bio is the project developer, technology provider, and market lead of the US
SecondCrop Microbial Carbon Removal project. Loam Bio produces and delivers the
microbial inoculant, supports farmers with practice adoption, and manages the soil
carbon project. The Loam Commercial Operation team oversees project enrollment and
eligibility verification, data collection and ongoing project monitoring, and facilitation of
credit issuance.
Estimated scale of carbon dioxide removal
It is estimated that the grouped project will achieve an average annual removal of 1.2
tonnes of CO2e per acre. Over the initial five-year crediting period, the project is
expected to remove approximately 126,745 tCO2e. By offering farmers a new, low-effort
tool to build stable soil carbon, the project aims to contribute significantly to climate
change mitigation while fostering more resilient and profitable agricultural practices. The
use of CarbonBuilder is a practice that meets additionality requirements without
requiring farmers to make extensive changes to their existing operations. These
outcomes underscore the project’s significant contribution to climate mitigation, while
also advancing sustainable and regenerative agricultural practices across the U.S.
Midwest.

Project Name: Small Farmers' Carbon Farming Project in Chishang Taiwan
Description:
Chishang, nestled in Taitung County, Taiwan, is renowned as a premier rice production region with a legacy of more than 70 years. The area’s lush paddies and traditional agricultural rhythms are stewarded by the Wanan Community Development Association (WACDA), an active local group established three decades ago. WACDA’s mission centers on environmental protection, cultural conservation, and the promotion of organic farming—principles that have guided community action and land management for generations.
Typical farming practices in Chishang after the second rice harvest in late October include thorough removal of litter and several rounds of tillage, followed by maintaining clear, fallow fields until February. This period of rest for the land helps sanitize soil-borne disease bacteria. However, to manage weeds during this time, herbicides have traditionally been applied, reflecting both pragmatic and conventional approaches to field management.
In recent years, Chishang’s farmers, under WACDA’s leadership, have begun to embrace more sustainable soil management techniques. Inspired by the Gold Standard for Global Goals (GS4GG) Soil Organic Carbon Framework Methodology, the community has adopted Cover Crops and Improved Tillage Activity Modules. These practices promise enhanced outcomes for both food security and climate resilience, by increasing soil carbon, reducing chemical use, and supporting ecosystem health.

Project Name: GS23487-Mangrove Restoration Project of 500 Hectares
Project Developer Name: IBERDROLA CARBON 2 NATURE MEXICO S.A DE C.V.
Description: The "Mangrove Restoration Project of 500 Hectares" is the first of several expected voluntary project activities (VPAs) to be included under the broader framework of the Programme of Activities (PoA) “Community Program for the Preservation and Restoration of Mangroves”. The project consists of the ecological restoration of 500.87 hectares of degraded mangrove ecosystems. The total project area consists of 500.87 ha, out of which 431.14 ha are eligible (degraded and transitional mangrove). Restoration measures include the reconnection of blocked tidal channels and removal of sediment plugs, assisted natural regeneration where hydrological conditions permit, and enrichment planting with native species.

Project Name: Community Program for the Preservation and Restoration of Mangroves
Project Developer Name: IBERDROLA CARBON 2 NATURE MEXICO S.A DE C.V.
Description:
The goal of the “Community Program for the Preservation and Restoration of Mangroves” is to implement projects for the restoration of degraded mangrove and coastal/freshwater wetlands ecosystems. The aim is to recover ecological functionality by re-establishing hydrological connectivity, improving biodiversity, and removing atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO₂) through natural carbon sequestration processes.
VPAs included under the PoA will be selected where degradation is demonstrated and restoration is technically feasible, prioritizing actions such as hydrological reconnection, assisted natural regeneration, and targeted enrichment planting with native species, together with the maintenance and monitoring required for durable outcomes. The aim is to restore ecological processes (water exchange, sediment dynamics, recruitment), enhance biodiversity, and remove CO₂ through conservative, verified sequestration in line with Gold Standard requirements.

Project Name: GS12793 GS12794 Mitti Labs: Supporting Climate Smart Rice Farming In Andhra Pradesh
Description: Mitti Labs plans to introduce Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) method to a group of around 12,000 farmers on 7,000 hectares in Andhra Pradesh, India. The project aims to avoid methane emissions related with the conventional farming (CF) techniques. The company, along with a local implementation partner Access Development Services; will provide advisory services to participating farmers about the implementation of AWD through training.

Project Name: GS23461-A/R project in Auvergne- Rhône Alpes and Grand-Est regions with Paulownia trees
Description:
The main goal of the project is to offer our customers the opportunity to invest with us to plant Paulownia trees in France (Auvergne Rhônes Alpes area) to capture a high quantity of CO2.
Regarding pay investment, Omega Green company buy or lease land, design all the servitudes required to optimize the yield (soil analyze, water loop irrigation, find compagnies to plant and maintain quality of the ground ,…). The goal is to deliver VER along the growth of trees.

Project Name: Uduma's Safe Water Project in Mali
Description: Uduma's Safe Water Project in Mali provides safe water to rural communities in Mali. Uduma installs low-emission water treatment technologies (in-line chlorination) to existing water supply systems, maintains the systems, and expands systems to reach those in need. The resulting safe water replaces the need to burn non-renewable biomass in water boiling for purification. This avoidance of fuel combustion reduces Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions and positively contributes to climate change goals and sustainable development goals.

Project Name: Uduma's Safe Water Program
Description:
Uduma's Safe Water Program provides safe water to rural communities in Western Africa with the first carbon project beginning in Mali.  Uduma installs low-emission water treatment technologies (in-line chlorination) to existing water supply systems, maintains the systems, and expands systems to reach those in need.  The resulting safe water replaces the need to burn non-renewable biomass in water boiling for purification.
This avoidance of fuel combustion reduces Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions and positively contributes to climate change goals and sustainable development goals.

Project Name: Electric Cooking Program in Bangladesh by ATEC VPA04
Description:
The Electric Cooking Program in Bangladesh by ATEC aims to replace highly polluting non-renewable and fossil cooking fuels with clean, efficient electric cooking solutions. The project will distribute and install ATEC’s patented eCook—an electric cookstove equipped with GSM connectivity that enables 100% digital MRV through ATEC’s dashboard. The primary beneficiaries are households that currently cook with biomass but are already connected to the national grid.

By transitioning to efficient electric cooking, the project will offer households an affordable, convenient, and safe cooking alternative while contributing to SDGs 1, 3, 5, 7, 8, and 13.

Project Description:
Distribution and installation of eCook units with GSM and 100% dMRV capabilities.
ATEC will lead project development and implementation and local implementation partners will support VPA management in Bangladesh.
The project is voluntary and will apply the Methodology for Metered & Measured Energy Cooking Devices v1.2 for calculating emission reductions.

The project will replace unsustainable biomass-based cooking—often practiced on inefficient three-stone fires and traditional stoves—with efficient electric cookstoves, thereby reducing GHG emissions and negative environmental and health impacts.

Project Name: GS13138 VPA1 Hygiene and Environmental Care for Tanks and Overhead Reservoirs (HECTOR) in Cambodia
Description: The purpose of the Voluntary Project Activity (VPA) is to provide safe drinking water to households in rural communities of Cambodia. This initiative intends to diminish the reliance on non-renewable biomass for water purification, which would otherwise be necessary in the absence of this project. The project aims to achieve this by deploying automated chlorination units, UV disinfection, and utilizing advanced IoT and AI-ML driven cloud technology for real-time water quality monitoring.

Project Name: Ecofoyer Guinée: Real-case VPA1 - Conakry N’Zérékoré Forécariah and Beyla
Description:
Carbon Neutral Energy Systems Limited (C-NES) has developed the Ecofoyer Guinée – Programme for the Dissemination of Improved and Advanced Cookstoves in Guinea as a Gold Standard Programme of Activities (PoA). The PoA reduces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and improve household well-being by displacing traditional, inefficient biomass cookstoves with modern, energy-efficient, and cleaner alternatives.

Through the dissemination of Improved Cookstoves (ICS), Advanced Cookstoves (ACS), and the local production of renewable biomass fuels (pellets), the Ecofoyer Guinée PoA contributes to sustainable energy access, climate change mitigation, and socio-economic development across urban, peri-urban, and rural areas of Guinea.

VPA 01, implemented under this PoA, covers the prefectures of Conakry, N’Zérékoré, Forécariah, and Beyla. The activity involves the dissemination, monitoring, and maintenance of ICS and ACS technologies, as well as the introduction of locally manufactured biomass pellets as a renewable fuel alternative. Implementation will be carried out through partnerships with Local Implementation Partners (LIPs) responsible for distribution, user training, awareness campaigns, and after-sales support.

This first VPA serves as the real-case example for the PoA, demonstrating the operational model, monitoring framework, and sustainable development co-benefits expected from future VPAs to be included under the Ecofoyer Guinée Programme.

Project Name: Ecofoyer Guinée - Programme for the Dissemination of Improved and Advanced Cookstoves in Guinea
Description:
Carbon Neutral Energy Systems Limited (C-NES) has developed the "Ecofoyer Guinée -  Programme for the Dissemination of Improved and Advanced Cookstoves in Guinea" as a registered Gold Standard Programme of Activities (PoA). The PoA reduces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and improves household well-being by displacing the use of traditional, inefficient biomass cookstoves with modern, energy-efficient and cleaner alternatives.

The PoA responds to Guinea’s NDCs and its significant dependence on traditional cooking practices, which has resulted in widespread deforestation, indoor air pollution, and associated health burdens—especially among women and children. Through the introduction of improved cookstoves (ICS), advanced cookstoves (ACS), and local manufacturing of renewable fuels (biomass pellets) the Ecofoyer Guinée PoA contributes to sustainable energy access, climate mitigation, and social development across urban, peri-urban and rural areas.

Under this country-wide PoA, the initial rollout includes the prefectures of Conakry, N’Zérékoré, Forécariah, and Beyla. The PoA involves the dissemination, monitoring, and maintenance of ICS and ACS technologies that meet defined efficiency and emission performance standards.

Project Name: FleetRefit Programme: VPA 1
FleetRefit Programme
Description: The Programme of Activities includes one or more energy efficiency retrofit measures implemented on ships to improve fuel efficiency and thereby reduce CO₂ emissions.

Project Name: Sustainable Cooking Solutions for Rural Communities: Madhya Pradesh Initiative
Description: Under this project activity, VISION GREENEN Tech Pvt Ltd is developing an improved cookstoves program “Sustainable Cooking Solutions for Rural Communities: Madhya Pradesh Initiative” and aims to distribute improved cookstoves (ICS) to rural households in the Dhar and Khargone districts of Madhya Pradesh.  The target beneficiaries of the program will be low-income & firewood-using households residing in villages of Dhar and Khargone districts of Madhya Pradesh. The Project intends to reach households that are currently using inefficient traditional cookstoves for cooking their meals. Households will be provided with improved cookstoves. ICS improves the combustion efficiency and thermal transfer to the cooking pot compared with a traditional stove, thereby reducing fuel usage. As a result, this project will contribute to SDGs including  SDG 3 (Good Health and Well Being), SDG 4 (Quality Education), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean energy), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), SDG 13 (Climate Action) and SDG 15 (Life on Land), in both quantitative and qualitative ways.

Project Name: Cova Safe Water Project - Honduras
Description: The VPA, "Cova Safe Water Project - Honduras," provides safe water to rural communities in Honduras.  Cova installs zero-emission water treatment technologies (in-line chlorination) to existing water supply systems.  The resulting safe water replaces the need to burn non-renewable biomass by end-users in water boiling for purification of water.  This avoidance of fuel combustion reduces Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions and contributes to climate change goals.

Project Name: Cova Safe Water Program in Central America
Description:
The Program of Activities, "Cova Safe Water Program in Central America" will provide safe water to rural communities in Central America with the first carbon project beginning in Honduras.  Cova installs zero-emission water treatment technologies (in-line chlorination) to existing water supply systems.  The resulting safe water replaces the need to burn non-renewable biomass in water boiling for purification of the water.
This avoidance of fuel combustion reduces Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions and contributes to climate change goals.

Project Name: Hygiene and Environmental Care for Tanks and Overhead Reservoirs (HECTOR) in Cambodia
Description:
The "Hygiene and Environmental Care for Tanks and Overhead Reservoirs (HECTOR) – Cambodia",  Programme of Activities (PoA) is a crucial initiative supporting Cambodia's goal of 100 percent access to improved water and sanitation in rural areas by 2025. With about 75 percent of Cambodians living in rural regions, limited access to safe water and sanitation is a pressing issue for public health and sustainable development. HECTOR addresses this challenge by introducing advanced water purification technologies to provide safe drinking water to rural households, thereby enhancing water quality, improving the quality of life for Cambodian communities, and reducing the environmental impact of traditional water treatment methods.
HECTOR integrates water tanks, automated chlorination units, UV disinfection, and IoT- and AI-ML-based real-time water quality monitoring, a modern approach that replaces conventional methods and reduces reliance on wood fuel and non-renewable biomass. As part of a larger Programme of Activities, HECTOR will expand these solutions to multiple locations across Cambodia, replicating its success through several Voluntary Project Activities (VPAs) with consistent technological applications for lasting impact.

Project Name: GS23468 VPA1 Hygiene and Environmental Care for Tanks and Overhead Reservoirs (HECTOR) in Ghana
Description: The purpose of the Voluntary Project Activity (VPA) is to provide safe drinking water to households in rural communities of Ghana. This initiative intends to diminish the reliance on non-renewable biomass for water purification, which would otherwise be necessary in the absence of this project. The project aims to achieve this by deploying automated chlorination units, UV disinfection, and utilizing advanced IoT and AI-ML driven cloud technology for real-time water quality monitoring.

Project Name: Hygiene and Environmental Care for Tanks and Overhead Reservoirs (HECTOR) in Ghana
Description: The purpose of the Programme of Activity (PoA) is to provide safe drinking water to households in rural communities. This initiative intends to diminish the reliance on non-renewable biomass for water purification, which would otherwise be necessary in the absence of this project. The project aims to achieve this by deploying automated chlorination units, UV disinfection, and utilizing advanced IoT and AI-ML driven cloud technology for real-time water quality monitoring. The geographical boundary and target are households in rural communities with overhead tank reservoirs that have no definitive chlorination or post UV disinfections. This project will provide online access via App to local inhabitants to monitor their water quality online, on their smart phones, and the distributed water will be free of charge to community. The project is anticipated to yield significant impacts in health, social, environmental, and economic spheres for thousands of individuals in the project area. Furthermore, this project is aligned with various UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to create a comprehensive positive impact.

Project Name: Uganda – Empower Communities Through Clean Energy-VPA 1
Description: The purpose of the programme is to provide end-users with energy- efficient technologies Solar lantern & Solar system with the aim to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Thus, the VPA supports the intended goals of reducing fuel consumption, improving health and reducing GHG emissions in Uganda.

Project Name: Nestle Biodigesters Program in India
Description: Sistema.bio is a social enterprise that provides access to innovative biodigester technology, training and financing to address the challenges of poverty, food security, and climate change. Sistema.bio manufactures and distributes high-quality, affordable biodigesters that enable farmers around the world to convert waste into energy and fertilizer, and works with farmers to ensure they become more sustainable, independent, and productive while mitigating the carbon emissions generated by agricultural activities. This project will provide a wide range of social, economic and environmental benefits for families and communities in India through the installation of biodigesters. The project includes various sizes of biodigesters to adjust to the different types of farmers. The project will focus on smallholder dairy farmers in the country, especially subsistence farmers who typically use woodfuel or LPG for their energy needs both in the household and productive uses in the farm, and who currently do not manage the manure generated by their animals and have either solid or liquid storage practices for this.

Project Name: Uganda – Empower Communities Through Clean Energy
Description: The purpose of the programme is to provide end-users with energy- efficient technologies Solar lantern & solar system with the aim to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Thus, the PoA supports the intended goals of reducing fuel consumption, improving health and reducing GHG emissions in Uganda.

Project Name: CCT Clean Cooking Project Tanzania
Description:
This Gold Standard Large-Scale (Individual) Project Activity is a voluntary initiative developed by Clean Cooking Technologies S.R.L. (CCT) as the Project Developer. The project will be supported by multiple investors and funding partners. The Host Country for this project is the United Republic of Tanzania, where the activity will be implemented across the country.

The project aims to distribute high-quality induction stoves and electric pressure cookers (collectively referred to as electric cookstoves) to households and communities in Tanzania. Implementation will be led by CCT Tanzania, in collaboration with local organizations. The project will initially be rolled out in Dar es Salaam and subsequently expanded to other regions across Tanzania based on demand.

In Tanzania, a large proportion of households continue to rely on inefficient traditional cooking methods—such as three-stone fires and traditional charcoal stoves—that consume significant amounts of biomass and contribute to deforestation and indoor air pollution. By introducing modern, energy-efficient electric cooking technologies, the project seeks to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, improve indoor air quality, and enhance the overall quality of life for participating households.

The project will be implemented in both rural and urban areas where households have access to electricity.  The project will have a total crediting period of 15 years, comprising an initial 5-year period with two renewals of 5 years each.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     100 of % distributed induction stoves in the project will be equipped with IoT devices to enable continuous monitoring of energy consumption for cooking and water heating in the households. The project will apply the Gold Standard-approved methodology, "Methodology for Metered & Measured Energy Cooking Devices" (Version 1.2), recognized as the most robust and conservative emission reduction calculation approach for cookstove carbon projects. This methodology is also endorsed by the ICVCM under its Core Carbon Principles (CCP), ensuring transparency and integrity in carbon credit issuance from cookstove projects.

By combining energy-efficient modern elelctric cookstove technology with real-time usage monitoring using the latest IoT device and a rigorous Gold Standard Metered Methodology, and further fNRB value derived from MoFUSS for Tanzania, the project will generate high-quality, high-integrity carbon credits while avoiding any risk of overestimation. The project is also one of the approved project by Sylvera.

Beyond emissions reduction, the project is designed to deliver substantial co-benefits aligned with many UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), including:
- SDG 1: No Poverty
- SDG 3: Good Health and Well-being
- SDG 4: Quality Education
- SDG 5: Gender Equality
- SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy
- SDG 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth
- SDG 12: Responsible Consumption and Production
- SDG 13: Climate Action
- SDG 15: Life on Land
These co-benefits will be formally certified under the Gold Standard framework, reinforcing the project's holistic impact on climate and community resilience.

Project Name: A/R project in France with Paulownia trees
Description:
The main goal of the project is to offer our customers the opportunity to invest with us to plant Paulownia trees to capture a high quantity of CO2.
Regarding pay investment, Omega Green company buy or lease land, design all the servitudes required to optimize the yield (soil analyze, water loop irrigation, find compagnies to plant and maintain quality of the ground ,…). And after we propose to our customer VER each year.

Project Name: Midnightsun PV Solar Park
Project Developer Name: GAİA CLİMATE FİNANSAL DANIŞMANLIK HİZMETLERİ VE TİCARET ANONİM ŞİRKETİ
Description: The Midnightsun PV Solar Park is a large-scale renewable energy initiative designed to support Jamaica's transition toward clean energy and contribute to the global effort to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The purpose of the project is to generate electricity by using clean solar power resources to meet the national electricity demand. The project will contribute to the reduction of GHG emission by displacing part with fossil fuel fired power plants of the generated electricity by the grid. The expected annual GHG emission reductions over the first crediting period are 78,879 tCO2e/yr, that will contribute to the alleviation of climate change effects. The Midnightsun Solar PV Project will contribute to the achievement of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) and SDG 13 (Climate Action).

Project Name: Community Initiated Regeneration for  Carbon and Livelihood Enhancement (CIRCLE) Project
Description: The primary objective of the Community Initiated Regeneration for Carbon & Livelihoods Enhancement (CIRCLE) project is carbon sequestration and livelihood enhancement through the restoration of degraded lands in Luapula province of Zambia. The project is expected to contribute to climate change mitigation and adaptation through the emission reductions and introduction of alternative livelihood skills across the project area respectively. The alternative livelihood activities are aimed at enhancing income generation among households and thereby reducing their propensity to engage in destructive activities such as charcoal production and conventional agriculture.
